topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of the waiting room felt cold beneath my fingertips. I kept adjusting the cuff of my borrowed sweater, the fabric suddenly too tight across my wrist. Every time the door clicked, a sharp little jolt ran through my chest, making me inhale a little too fast. I watched the receptionist, a woman with an unnervingly calm demeanor, and felt my shoulders involuntarily hunch. This was it. Meeting Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my confidence with a smirk and a well-aimed comment. Now, he was supposed to be… healing people. My palms were slick against my jeans as I waited for the familiar, irritatingly charming voice to cut through the quiet.

[story 2]

I gripped the strap of my messenger bag until my knuckles were white. The fluorescent lights of the clinic hummed, a high, incessant whine that grated on my nerves. I kept glancing at the closed door, my gaze darting back and forth as if expecting an ambush. My jaw felt locked, a tight band of muscle refusing to relax. When Mark finally emerged, leaning against the doorframe with an easy, practiced grace, I felt a sudden, unwelcome wave of panic. His eyes, the same sharp, assessing blue I remembered, met mine, and for a fleeting second, I saw something flicker in them—recognition, perhaps, or just a practiced neutrality that made my stomach clench.

[story 3]

The air in the consultation room was thick, heavy with the scent of sandalwood and something faintly metallic. I sat ramrod straight on the edge of the leather chair, my breathing shallow and quick. I meticulously smoothed the lapel of my jacket, a repetitive motion designed to anchor me against the swirling uncertainty inside. When Mark finally settled opposite me, his expression was one of studied empathy, a mask I knew intimately from years of playground confrontations. His voice, when he began to speak, was low and measured, and every carefully chosen word seemed to land with deliberate weight, making my insides feel strangely rigid, unable to shift or breathe freely.
